what: Starting point: a raw event log
title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: practice, interaction data often arrives single table one row per event. row records actor performed action, action , occurred. simulate Human–AI tutoring interaction actor four distinct codes: can also combine single event log actor column, data often arrives form:
code: library(htna) library(Nestimate)  set.seed(123)  human_codes <- c("Ask", "Confirm", "Revise", "Evaluate") ai_codes    <- c("Suggest", "Explain", "Execute", "Clarify") sessions    <- paste0("S", sprintf("%03d", 1:30))  make_events <- function(codes, sessions, parity) {   rows <- lapply(sessions, function(sid) {     n <- sample(15:25, 1)     data.frame(       session_id       = sid,       code             = sample(codes, n, replace = TRUE),       order_in_session = seq_len(n) * 2L - parity,       stringsAsFactors = FALSE     )   })   do.call(rbind, rows) }  human_data <- make_events(human_codes, sessions, 1L) ai_data    <- make_events(ai_codes, sessions, 0L) events <- rbind(   data.frame(actor = "Human", human_data, stringsAsFactors = FALSE),   data.frame(actor = "AI", ai_data, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) ) events <- events[order(events$session_id, events$order_in_session), ] head(events[, c("actor", "session_id", "code", "order_in_session")]) #>     actor session_id    code order_in_session #> 1   Human       S001 Confirm                1 #> 616    AI       S001 Explain                2 #> 2   Human       S001  Revise                3 #> 617    AI       S001 Execute                4 #> 3   Human       S001 Confirm                5 #> 618    AI       S001 Execute                6

what: Option 1: Single data frame with an actor column
title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: data already long format session order columns, can pass directly build_htna() using actor_col argument:

what: Option 2: Named list of data frames
title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: data actor stored separately (prepared independently), pass named list:

what: Option 3: Prepare each actor with Nestimate::prepare()
title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: raw data needs cleaning – e.g. session detection timestamps, tie-breaking, format conversion – can use Nestimate::prepare() actor’s data independently, feed prepared long-format data build_htna(). prepare() returns list several components. long_data element contains cleaned long-format data ready network estimation: Pass prepared long data named list:
code: prep_human <- prepare(   human_data,   actor   = "session_id",   action  = "code",   session = "session_id",   order   = "order_in_session" )  prep_ai <- prepare(   ai_data,   actor   = "session_id",   action  = "code",   session = "session_id",   order   = "order_in_session" ) head(prep_human$long_data[, c("session_id", "code", "order_in_session")]) #>   session_id    code order_in_session #> 1       S001 Confirm                1 #> 2       S001  Revise                3 #> 3       S001 Confirm                5 #> 4       S001 Confirm                7 #> 5       S001 Confirm                9 #> 6       S001  Revise               11 net3 <- build_htna(   list(Human = prep_human$long_data, AI = prep_ai$long_data) ) net3 #> Transition Network (relative probabilities) [directed] #>   Weights: [0.006, 0.308]  |  mean: 0.140 #>  #>   Weight matrix: #>              Ask Clarify Confirm Evaluate Execute Explain Revise Suggest #>   Ask      0.013   0.248   0.013    0.040   0.215   0.275  0.013   0.181 #>   Clarify  0.273   0.045   0.240    0.214   0.013   0.032  0.175   0.006 #>   Confirm  0.024   0.183   0.012    0.006   0.268   0.207  0.018   0.280 #>   Evaluate 0.021   0.210   0.035    0.007   0.308   0.189  0.007   0.224 #>   Execute  0.191   0.013   0.270    0.230   0.013   0.013  0.257   0.013 #>   Explain  0.194   0.035   0.215    0.229   0.021   0.028  0.271   0.007 #>   Revise   0.014   0.295   0.007    0.027   0.199   0.226  0.021   0.212 #>   Suggest  0.250   0.014   0.293    0.186   0.014   0.021  0.193   0.029  #>  #>   Initial probabilities: #>   Ask           0.300  ████████████████████████████████████████ #>   Revise        0.267  ████████████████████████████████████ #>   Evaluate      0.233  ███████████████████████████████ #>   Confirm       0.200  ███████████████████████████ #>   Clarify       0.000   #>   Execute       0.000   #>   Explain       0.000   #>   Suggest       0.000 plot_htna(net3, layout = "auto")

title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: three approaches produce network underlying data equivalent. Choose whichever fits data pipeline.

title: Data Preparation for HTNA
text: different actors share code labels (e.g. Human AI code called “Ask”), build_htna() raise error default. Set disambiguate = TRUE automatically prefix codes actor name:
code: net <- build_htna(data, actor_col = "actor", disambiguate = TRUE) # Codes become "Human:Ask", "AI:Ask", etc.

title: Getting Started with htna
text: Start loading package example data shipped Nestimate: build_htna() accepts named list long-format data frames, one per actor group. combines sequences, estimates transition probabilities, stores actor partition resulting network object: actor partition available two forms: net$nodes$groups – actor label per node (auto-detected plot_htna()). net$node_groups – canonical (node, group) data frame used cograph::get_groups() cograph::cluster_summary().

title: Getting Started with htna
text: plot_htna() auto-detects actor groups renders distinct colours. defaults circular layout 0.05 edge threshold:
code: plot_htna(net)

title: Getting Started with htna
text: Meta-paths type-level patterns abstract away concrete states ask often typed template appears data. example, Human->AI->Human captures every length-3 path starts Human code, transitions AI code, returns Human code. can search specific schema (wildcards): Gapped meta-paths allow skipping positions: Filter lift find -represented meta-paths:
code: # Enumerate all meta-paths of length 2 to 4 mp <- extract_meta_paths(net) mp #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 28 | Lengths: 2, 3, 4 | Gaps: 0 #>            schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>         Human->AI      2   0  5970   428   0.998     0.316 1.28 #>         AI->Human      2   0  5693   424   0.988     0.301 1.22 #>      Human->Human      2   0  4674   422   0.984     0.247 0.79 #>            AI->AI      2   0  2581   403   0.939     0.136 0.70 #>  Human->AI->Human      3   0  3593   402   0.937     0.194 1.41 #>  Human->Human->AI      3   0  3172   422   0.984     0.172 1.25 #>  AI->Human->Human      3   0  2828   403   0.939     0.153 1.11 #>     AI->Human->AI      3   0  2744   383   0.893     0.148 1.36 #>     Human->AI->AI      3   0  2189   403   0.939     0.118 1.09 #>     AI->AI->Human      3   0  2100   397   0.925     0.114 1.04 #> ... (18 more) # Any length-3 path that returns to Human extract_meta_paths(net, schema = "Human->*->Human") #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 2 | Lengths: 3 | Gaps: 0 #>               schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>     Human->AI->Human      3   0  3593   402   0.937     0.709 1.41 #>  Human->Human->Human      3   0  1472   396   0.923     0.291 0.46 extract_meta_paths(net, length = 3, type = "gapped", gap = 1:2) #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 16 | Lengths: 3 | Gaps: 1, 2 #>               schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>     Human->AI->Human      3   1  3265   408   0.951     0.185 1.35 #>     Human->Human->AI      3   1  2722   413   0.963     0.154 1.12 #>        AI->Human->AI      3   1  2515   379   0.883     0.143 1.31 #>     AI->Human->Human      3   1  2242   391   0.911     0.127 0.92 #>  Human->Human->Human      3   1  2146   372   0.867     0.122 0.70 #>        Human->AI->AI      3   1  1748   384   0.895     0.099 0.91 #>        AI->AI->Human      3   1  1618   360   0.839     0.092 0.84 #>           AI->AI->AI      3   1  1382   304   0.709     0.078 0.91 #>  Human->Human->Human      3   2  2948   399   0.930     0.176 1.01 #>     Human->Human->AI      3   2  2341   390   0.909     0.139 1.01 #> ... (6 more) extract_meta_paths(net, length = 3, min_lift = 1.2) #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 3 | Lengths: 3 | Gaps: 0 #>            schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>  Human->AI->Human      3   0  3593   402   0.937     0.194 1.41 #>  Human->Human->AI      3   0  3172   422   0.984     0.172 1.25 #>     AI->Human->AI      3   0  2744   383   0.893     0.148 1.36

title: Multi-Group HTNA (3+ Actor Groups)
text: simulate collaborative coding session three actors – Student, AI assistant, System (e.g. IDE automated tooling) – four distinct codes:
code: library(htna)  set.seed(42)  student_codes <- c("Ask", "Confirm", "Revise", "Evaluate") ai_codes      <- c("Suggest", "Explain", "Execute", "Clarify") system_codes  <- c("Log", "Notify", "Compile", "Sync") sessions      <- paste0("S", sprintf("%03d", 1:30))  make_events <- function(codes, sessions, offset) {   rows <- lapply(sessions, function(sid) {     n <- sample(15:25, 1)     data.frame(       session_id       = sid,       code             = sample(codes, n, replace = TRUE),       order_in_session = seq_len(n) * 3L - offset,       stringsAsFactors = FALSE     )   })   do.call(rbind, rows) }  student_data <- make_events(student_codes, sessions, 2L) ai_data      <- make_events(ai_codes, sessions, 1L) system_data  <- make_events(system_codes, sessions, 0L)

title: Multi-Group HTNA (3+ Actor Groups)
text: Pass three groups named list: actor partition now contains three levels:

title: Multi-Group HTNA (3+ Actor Groups)
text: three groups, plot_htna() automatically switches polygon (triangle) layout. three colours default HTNA palette applied order:  can switch layouts well:
code: plot_htna(net3) plot_htna(net3, layout = "auto")

title: Multi-Group HTNA (3+ Actor Groups)
text: Meta-paths now span three actor types. example, can search paths System mediates Student AI: enumerate length-3 meta-paths:
code: extract_meta_paths(net3, schema = "Student->System->AI") #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 30 sequences #> (no rows met the filters) extract_meta_paths(net3, length = 3) #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 30 sequences #> Rows: 17 | Lengths: 3 | Gaps: 0 #>                    schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>       Student->AI->System      3   0   510    30   1.000     0.295 7.98 #>       AI->System->Student      3   0   497    30   1.000     0.288 7.78 #>       System->Student->AI      3   0   487    30   1.000     0.282 7.62 #>            AI->System->AI      3   0    41    10   0.333     0.024 0.62 #>        System->AI->System      3   0    37    10   0.333     0.021 0.56 #>   System->Student->System      3   0    32    10   0.333     0.019 0.50 #>  Student->System->Student      3   0    27     9   0.300     0.016 0.44 #>      Student->AI->Student      3   0    24     7   0.233     0.014 0.39 #>           AI->Student->AI      3   0    24     7   0.233     0.014 0.38 #>    System->System->System      3   0    16     7   0.233     0.009 0.24 #> ... (7 more)

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Start loading package example data shipped Nestimate: Build heterogeneous transition network:

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Calling plot_htna() extra arguments uses htna defaults: circular layout, 0.05 edge threshold, built-colour palette.
code: plot_htna(m) #> Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'cograph': #>   method             from      #>   plot.net_stability Nestimate #>   print.mcml         Nestimate #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: layout parameter controls two groups arranged space.

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Places group vertical column, cross-group edges drawn . classic bipartite layout.
code: plot_htna(m, layout = "bipartite") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Distributes nodes along perimeter circle, alternating groups. Good emphasising overall connectivity pattern.
code: plot_htna(m, layout = "circular") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: orientation parameter rotates rearranges bipartite layout. applies layout = "bipartite" ("auto" 2 groups).

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Groups placed two vertical columns, side side.
code: plot_htna(m, orientation = "vertical") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Groups stacked two horizontal rows, one .
code: plot_htna(m, orientation = "horizontal") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Similar vertical, two columns face nodes aligned rank, making easier trace individual edges.
code: plot_htna(m, orientation = "facing") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Arranges group along semicircle, groups together form full circle.
code: plot_htna(m, orientation = "circular") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: group_spacing parameter controls horizontal distance two columns. Larger values push groups apart, can help reduce edge overlap dense networks.
code: plot_htna(m, group_spacing = 1) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, group_spacing = 12) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: node_spacing parameter controls vertical gap nodes within group. Increase node labels overlap; decrease compact plot.
code: plot_htna(m, node_spacing = 0.3) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, node_spacing = 1.5) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: columns parameter splits group multiple sub-columns. useful group many nodes single column tall. scalar applies groups; length-2 vector sets group independently.
code: plot_htna(m, columns = 2) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, columns = c(1, 2)) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: using multiple columns, column_spacing adjusts horizontal gap sub-columns within group.
code: plot_htna(m, columns = 2, column_spacing = 0.5) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: default (use_list_order = TRUE), nodes appear order found data. Setting FALSE reorders nodes edge weight, placing connected nodes centre group.
code: plot_htna(m, use_list_order = FALSE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: curvature parameter controls much cross-group edges bend. 0 produces straight lines, higher values increase arc. Useful reducing overlapping edges.
code: plot_htna(m, curvature = 0) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, curvature = 1) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: intra_curvature parameter adds extra curvature edges nodes group, arcing away opposite group. visually separates within-group -group transitions.
code: plot_htna(m, intra_curvature = 0.5) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: vector colours, one per group. recommended approach scales number groups.
code: plot_htna(m, group_colors = c("red", "purple")) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: parameters still work two-group networks generalise three groups.
code: plot_htna(m, group1_color = "green", group2_color = "darkred") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: default, edge colours derived (darkened) group colours source node.

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Supply vector colours override automatic derivation.
code: plot_htna(m, edge_colors = c("red", "blue")) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Set edge_colors = FALSE use single default colour edges.
code: plot_htna(m, edge_colors = FALSE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: group_shapes parameter assigns different node shape group, adding second visual channel beyond colour. Common shapes include "circle", "square", "diamond", "triangle".
code: plot_htna(m, group_shapes = c("diamond", "triangle")) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
code: plot_htna(m, group1_shape = "triangle", group2_shape = "diamond") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Jitter shifts nodes horizontally based connectivity, breaking rigid column alignment. Nodes stronger cross-group edges move closer group, giving visual indication coupling strength.
code: plot_htna(m, jitter = TRUE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: numeric value controls maximum displacement.
code: plot_htna(m, jitter = 0.5) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: default, first group jittered. Set jitter_side = "" apply jitter groups.
code: plot_htna(m, jitter = TRUE, jitter_side = "both") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
code: plot_htna(m, jitter = TRUE, jitter_side = "second") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
code: plot_htna(m, legend = FALSE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: legend_position parameter accepts standard R positions: "bottomright" (default), "bottomleft", "topleft", "topright".
code: plot_htna(m, legend_position = "topleft") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, legend_position = "topright") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Draw extension lines outward node, can help connect labels nodes dense plots. TRUE uses default length; numeric value sets length explicitly.
code: plot_htna(m, extend_lines = TRUE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, extend_lines = 0.3) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: layout_margin parameter adds padding around plot area. Increase labels clipped edges.
code: plot_htna(m, layout_margin = 0) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, layout_margin = 0.4) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: scale parameter adjusts spacing proportionally, useful exporting higher resolutions. value 2 doubles distances.
code: plot_htna(m, scale = 2) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: label_abbrev parameter truncates node labels fixed number characters (numeric) lets function choose automatically ("auto"). Helpful code names long.
code: plot_htna(m, label_abbrev = 3) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups plot_htna(m, label_abbrev = "auto") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Pass data frame label column (matching) labels column (display) rename nodes plot without modifying network.
code: nodes_df <- data.frame(   label  = c("left", "right"),   labels = paste0("N", 1:10) ) plot_htna(m, nodes = nodes_df) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: angle_spacing parameter controls empty space corners circular polygon layouts. Higher values create larger gaps vertices, visually separating groups.
code: plot_htna(m, layout = "circular", angle_spacing = 0.3) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: additional arguments forwarded cograph::splot() via .... gives access full set rendering options.

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: threshold parameter (passed splot) hides edges weights given value.
code: plot_htna(m, threshold = 0.1) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
code: plot_htna(m, edge.labels = FALSE) #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: plot_htna: 2-Group Parameter Gallery
text: Parameters can freely combined fine-tune visualisation:
code: plot_htna(m,           group_colors = c("#E91E63", "#009688"),           group_shapes = c("circle", "diamond"),           group_spacing = 6,           jitter = TRUE,           jitter_side = "both",           curvature = 0.6,           threshold = 0.08,           legend_position = "topright") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: Authors and Citation

code: @Manual{,   title = {htna: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis},   author = {Mohammed Saqr},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/mohsaqr/htna}, }

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: htna R package Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis (HTNA) models processes interactions mix two actor groups (e.g.  Human AI) single network. HTNA builds traditions Transition Network Analysis (TNA) Co-occurrence Network Analysis (CNA) maintains rigor either method. package provides focused API top Nestimate estimation engine cograph rendering engine: build network combined sequence preserving actor partition, downstream bootstrap, permutation, reliability, centrality, plotting functions treat actor’s codes distinct node group.

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: can install development version htna GitHub:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("sonsoleslp/htna")

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: Load example data shipped Nestimate:
code: library("htna") data(human_long, ai_long, package = "Nestimate")

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: build_htna() takes named list long-format data frames, one per actor group, combines sequences, estimates transition probabilities, stores actor partition resulting network:

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: plot_htna() auto-detects actor groups renders distinct colours:
code: plot_htna(net, threshold = 0.05, layout = "circular") #> Using 'groups' column for node groups

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: Meta-paths type-level patterns abstract away concrete states. example, Human->AI->Human captures every length-3 path starts Human code, transitions AI code, returns Human code: Search specific schema wildcards:
code: extract_meta_paths(net) #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 28 | Lengths: 2, 3, 4 | Gaps: 0 #>            schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>         Human->AI      2   0  5970   428   0.998     0.316 1.28 #>         AI->Human      2   0  5693   424   0.988     0.301 1.22 #>      Human->Human      2   0  4674   422   0.984     0.247 0.79 #>            AI->AI      2   0  2581   403   0.939     0.136 0.70 #>  Human->AI->Human      3   0  3593   402   0.937     0.194 1.41 #>  Human->Human->AI      3   0  3172   422   0.984     0.172 1.25 #>  AI->Human->Human      3   0  2828   403   0.939     0.153 1.11 #>     AI->Human->AI      3   0  2744   383   0.893     0.148 1.36 #>     Human->AI->AI      3   0  2189   403   0.939     0.118 1.09 #>     AI->AI->Human      3   0  2100   397   0.925     0.114 1.04 #> ... (18 more) extract_meta_paths(net, schema = "Human->*->Human") #> Meta-paths (type-level) over 429 sequences #> Rows: 2 | Lengths: 3 | Gaps: 0 #>               schema length gap count n_seq support frequency lift #>     Human->AI->Human      3   0  3593   402   0.937     0.709 1.41 #>  Human->Human->Human      3   0  1472   396   0.923     0.291 0.46

title: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis
text: tna – Transition Network Analysis homogeneous sequences. Nestimate – Network estimation, bootstrap, permutation, reliability, centrality. cograph – Network visualisation rendering. codyna – Sequence patterns, outcomes, indices.

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
text: Thin wrapper around Nestimate::bootstrap_network() runs standard state-level edge bootstrap htna network tags result identifiable htna bootstrap. bootstrap inference Nestimate's: per-edge mean, sd, p-value, significance, confidence / credibility intervals across iter resamples. State-level granularity correct level edge-stability analysis - aggregating actor pairs smear real edge--edge differences.

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
code: bootstrap_htna(x, ...)

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
text: x [htna] network built build_htna(). ... Arguments forwarded Nestimate::bootstrap_network() (e.g. iter, ci_level, consistency_range, seed).

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
text: object class c("htna_bootstrap", "net_bootstrap"). slots produced Nestimate::bootstrap_network() preserved verbatim ($summary, $mean, $sd, $p_values, $significant, $ci_lower/$ci_upper, $cr_lower/$cr_upper, $model, $original, etc.).

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
text: wrapper adds identity downstream class continuity: returned object class "htna_bootstrap" ahead "net_bootstrap", inherits(boot, "htna_bootstrap") works. actor partition ($nodes$groups, $node_groups) restored boot$model input network cograph's auto-detect still recognizes heterogeneous schema downstream. boot$model re-tagged "htna" front class chain chain c("htna", "netobject", "cograph_network") - identical build_htna() returns.

title: Bootstrap an HTNA Network — bootstrap_htna
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(human_long, ai_long, package = "Nestimate") net  <- build_htna(list(Human = human_long, AI = ai_long)) boot <- bootstrap_htna(net, iter = 200) inherits(boot, "htna_bootstrap")            # TRUE inherits(boot$model, "htna")                # TRUE head(boot$summary)                          # state-level edge stability } # }

what: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
title: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
text: Builds transition network combined sequence two actor groups (e.g. Human AI) preserves actor partition result downstream plotting analysis can treat actor's codes distinct node group.

title: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
code: build_htna(   data,   actor_col = NULL,   action = "code",   session = "session_id",   order = "order_in_session",   method = "relative",   disambiguate = FALSE,   ... )

title: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
text: data Either: named list long-format data frames, one per actor (e.g. list(Human = human_long, AI = ai_long)). frames must share column schema. single long-format data frame actor column. case actor_col must supplied. actor_col Character. Name actor column data single data frame. Ignored data named list. action Character. Name action/code column. Default "code". session Character. Name session column. Default "session_id". order Character. Name within-session order column. Default "order_in_session". method Character. Transition method passed build_network: "relative" (default), "frequency", "attention". disambiguate Logical. FALSE (default), function errors code label appears one actor group. TRUE, codes prefixed actor name ("Human:Ask", "AI:Ask") become distinct nodes. ... Additional arguments forwarded build_network.

title: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
text: netobject actor partition stored cograph's canonical schema, cograph::plot_htna(net) auto-detects groups arguments: $nodes$groups - actor label per node (column name cograph::plot_htna auto-detects). $node_groups - data frame columns node group (canonical cograph_network schema, also readable cograph::get_groups, cluster_summary, print method cograph_network). slots exactly returned build_network.

title: Build a Heterogeneous Transition Network (HTNA) — build_htna
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(human_long, ai_long, package = "Nestimate") net <- build_htna(list(Human = human_long, AI = ai_long)) net$node_groups            # canonical (node, group) schema cograph::plot_htna(net)    # auto-detects $nodes$groups, no other args } # }

what: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
text: Discovers type-level patterns (meta-paths) actor-typed sequences produced heterogeneous transition network. meta-path fixed sequence node types - example Human->AI->Human - abstracts away concrete states asks often typed template appears data. Two regimes supported: enumerate every meta-path given lengths (default), search one specific schema (optional wildcards).

title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
code: extract_meta_paths(   x,   length = 2:4,   schema = NULL,   type = c("contiguous", "gapped"),   gap = 1L,   min_count = 1L,   min_support = 0,   min_lift = 0,   start = NULL,   end = NULL,   contain = NULL )

title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
text: x [htna_network] network built build_htna(). Must $nodes$groups, $node_groups, $data populated. length [integer(): 2:4] Meta-path lengths enumerate. Ignored schema supplied. schema [character(1)] Optional. meta-path template search , written actor names separated "->" (e.g. "Human->AI->Human"). Wildcards supported: "Human->*->Human" matches every length-3 meta-path starts ends Human, type . type [character(1): "contiguous"]"contiguous" (default) considers consecutive positions; "gapped" considers positions spaced gap + 1 apart. gap [integer(): 1L] Gap size(s) used type = "gapped". Multiple values produce one row per (length, gap) combination, distinguished gap column. min_count [integer(1): 1L] Minimum total occurrences retain. min_support [numeric(1): 0] Minimum proportion sequences containing meta-path least . min_lift [numeric(1): 0] Minimum lift (observed / expected marginal independence). 0 disables filter. start [character()] Keep meta-paths whose first type set. end [character()] Keep meta-paths whose last type set. contain [character()] Keep meta-paths whose type sequence contains elements set (order).

title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
text: object class c("htna_meta_paths", "htna_paths", "data.frame") columns: schema, length, gap, count, n_sequences, support, frequency, lift. Attributes: n_sequences, alphabet, level = "meta".

title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
text: Operates actor partition stored network build_htna().

title: Extract Meta-Paths from a Heterogeneous Transition Network — extract_meta_paths
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(human_long, ai_long, package = "Nestimate") net <- build_htna(list(Human = human_long, AI = ai_long))  # All meta-paths of length 2..4 extract_meta_paths(net)  # Wildcard family search: any length-3 path that returns to Human extract_meta_paths(net, schema = "Human->*->Human")  # Gapped meta-paths extract_meta_paths(net, length = 3, type = "gapped", gap = 1:2)  # Lift threshold (over-represented relative to type marginals) extract_meta_paths(net, length = 3, min_lift = 1.2) } # }

what: htna: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis — htna-package
title: htna: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis — htna-package
text: Build, analyze, visualize transition networks sequences mix two actor groups (e.g. Human AI) within interaction.

title: htna: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis — htna-package
text: package thin layer top two foundations: Nestimate - estimation, bootstrap, permutation, reliability, centrality, Markov machinery. cograph - rendering (splot, plot_htna, transitions, communities, chord, heatmap). htna adds heterogeneous-actor wrapper build_htna re-exports commonly used downstream functions users need attach Nestimate cograph explicitly.

title: htna: Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis — htna-package
text: Maintainer: Mohammed Saqr saqr@saqr.[copyright holder]
